originalTitle: New outbreak of Ebola kills 65 in eastern DR Congo
title: Helping Hands in DR Congo: Keeping Everyone Healthy!
content:
Hi everyone! Your friend, Reporter Fifi, is here with some important news from our amazing continent, Africa!

A Tiny Germ Called Ebola

Sometimes, tiny, tiny germs can make people sick. You can't even see these germs because they are super-duper small! One of these germs is called Ebola. When someone gets Ebola, they can feel very, very unwell. It's like having a very bad flu, but much worse.

Did you know? Germs are everywhere! Most germs are harmless, but some, like Ebola, can make us sick if they get inside our bodies.

Where is DR Congo?

The news today is from a beautiful country in Africa called the Democratic Republic of Congo, or DR Congo for short. It's in the middle of our continent, with lots of green forests and big rivers. Many wonderful children just like you live there!

Did you know? Africa is a huge continent with 54 different countries, each with its own special stories and people!

What Happened?

Sadly, some people in DR Congo recently got sick from the Ebola germ. Our big doctors and health experts in Africa told us that about 246 people felt unwell because of this sickness. And with a very sad heart, we know that some people, about 65 of them, couldn't get better. This makes everyone sad, but it also makes doctors and helpers work even harder to make sure more people get well and stay safe.

When lots of people get sick from the same germ, we call it an 'outbreak.' It means we need to be extra careful and help each other.

How Does Ebola Make You Feel?

Imagine having a very bad tummy ache, a hot head (that's called a fever), and feeling very tired. That's how someone with Ebola might feel. Their body might ache all over. It's a very difficult sickness, which is why doctors and nurses rush to help.

If you ever feel very sick, it's super important to tell a grown-up right away! They will help you find a doctor.

How Does Ebola Spread?

Good news! Ebola does not fly through the air like a sneeze or a cough. You can't catch it just by being near someone. Ebola spreads if you touch someone who is very sick with it, especially their blood or other body fluids. That's why doctors and nurses wear special suits, gloves, and masks. They are like superheroes in their protective gear!

Did you know? Washing your hands with soap and water is one of the best ways to get rid of many kinds of germs and stay healthy! It's like having magic cleaning powers!

Our Amazing Health Heroes!

There are so many brave people helping in DR Congo! Doctors, nurses, and health workers are working day and night to take care of the sick people. They give them medicines and help them feel as comfortable as possible. They also teach everyone in the villages how to stay safe and healthy.

These heroes are working really hard to stop the Ebola germ from making more people sick. They are showing great courage and kindness every single day.

Did you know? Health workers are like real-life superheroes! They learn a lot about how our bodies work and how to fix them when they get broken or sick.

How YOU Can Be a Helper!

Even though you are small, you can be a big helper in keeping everyone healthy! Here are some simple but super important things you can do:

  • Wash Your Hands! Use soap and clean water to wash your hands often, especially before eating and after using the toilet. Sing a happy song while you wash for at least 20 seconds!
  • Tell a Grown-Up! If you ever feel sick, even a little bit, tell your mom, dad, teacher, or another grown-up you trust. They will know what to do.
  • Keep Clean! Help keep your home and school clean. Clean spaces help keep germs away.
  • Be Kind! Remember to be kind to everyone. Sometimes people can get scared, and being kind makes everyone feel better.

Working Together with Uganda

DR Congo has a neighbour country called Uganda. Sometimes, people travel between countries. That's why one person in Uganda also got sick with Ebola. But don't worry! The health heroes in Uganda are also working very hard to help that person get better and to make sure no more people get sick there.

Countries in Africa are working together like good friends to help each other during this time. They share important information and send helpers where they are needed most.

Did you know? Working together is always stronger! When we help our neighbours, everyone becomes safer and happier.

Always Learning, Always Helping!

Remember, many people are working very hard to make sure everyone in DR Congo and Uganda stays safe and healthy. They are learning more about Ebola every day, and they know how to help people get better. By washing our hands and listening to our grown-ups, we can all do our part to keep our communities strong and healthy!

That's all for today from Reporter Fifi! Stay safe, stay healthy, and keep being amazing!
summary: Brave doctors and health workers are helping people in DR Congo and Uganda get well from a sickness called Ebola, and washing our hands helps everyone stay safe.
whyMatters: It's important to know about Ebola so we can help each other stay healthy and safe by washing our hands, keeping clean, and listening to our grown-ups.

originalTitle: Former Nigerian minister sentenced to 75 years in rare corruption verdict
title: A Big Story About Being Fair in Nigeria!
content:
Hello, super friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, ready with a very important story from a big country in Africa called Nigeria. This story is all about being fair, doing the right thing, and why rules are important for everyone!

Meet Mr. Saleh Mamman

There once was a man named Mr. Saleh Mamman. He used to have a very, very important job in Nigeria. Imagine your school principal or the head of your village – they help lots of people, right? Well, Mr. Mamman was like a super-duper helper for the whole country! He was a 'Minister of Power'.

What is a Minister?

A Minister is someone who helps run a part of the country. Each Minister looks after something important. One might look after schools, another might look after hospitals, and another might look after roads. Mr. Mamman's job was to look after 'Power' for Nigeria. That's a really big and important job!

What is 'Power'?

When we say 'Power', we don't mean superpowers like your favorite cartoon hero! We mean **electricity**! Have you ever seen the lights in your classroom? That's electricity! When you watch TV at home, or listen to music on a radio, or charge your mum's phone, that's all thanks to electricity. It helps cook food, keeps our homes cool with fans when it's hot, and even helps doctors in hospitals take care of sick people. Imagine a school without lights, or a shop without a fridge to keep food fresh. Electricity makes so many things possible and helps everyone live better lives. So, Mr. Mamman's job was to make sure that electricity was there for everyone in Nigeria, and that the money for it was used wisely. Did you know? Nigeria is a very big country in West Africa, with lots and lots of people! It's known for its colorful clothes and lively music.

A Big Problem: What is 'Corruption'?

Now, let's talk about a big word: **corruption**. It sounds tricky, but it just means when someone in an important job does something unfair with money that belongs to everyone. Imagine your teacher gives you money to buy new chalk and books for the whole class. But then, you take some of that money and buy sweets just for yourself, and there isn't enough left for the chalk and books. That wouldn't be fair, right? That's a bit like what corruption is. It's when people who are supposed to look after money for schools, hospitals, or electricity, take some of it for themselves. This means there isn't enough money to help everyone, and things like electricity might not work as well as they should. It makes people sad when there are blackouts and no power because the money meant for fixing things was taken.

The Judge Said So

When someone is accused of doing something wrong, special people called 'judges' listen to all the clues and evidence. They try to find out the truth. In this story, a special judge in Nigeria looked at all the clues about Mr. Mamman. They listened to everyone who had something to say. After looking at everything very carefully, the judge found out that Mr. Mamman did this bad thing. He was 'convicted'. That means the judge said, "Yes, he did it. He was not fair. He used the money wrongly." Did you know? Judges wear special black robes in court to show they are important and fair.

A Very, Very Long Time!

Because it was a very serious problem – because taking money meant for electricity hurts many, many people – the judge gave a very, very big punishment. Mr. Mamman was 'sentenced' to 75 years. Can you imagine how long 75 years is? It's much, much longer than you or your parents have been alive! It's a super long time, and it shows how serious it is when someone in an important job does something wrong with money that belongs to everyone.

But Where Is He? The Mystery!

Here's a bit of a mystery, friends! Even though the judge said Mr. Mamman should be punished for a very long time, nobody knows where Mr. Mamman is right now. He is missing! The people who are supposed to find him and make sure he faces his punishment are still looking for him. It's like a game of hide-and-seek, but a very serious one. Did you know? In Nigeria, many children love to play football, just like children all over Africa!

Why This Story is Important for All of Us

This story from Nigeria teaches us a very important lesson: **rules are for everyone**. Even big helpers like Ministers must follow the rules. It shows that being fair and honest is super important, especially when you are looking after things for lots of other people. When people are corrupt, it makes things harder for everyone. Schools might not get lights, hospitals might not get the power they need, and homes might be dark. Good leaders and good people make sure money is used to help everyone, not just themselves. They are honest and always try to do what is best for their community and country. Did you know? Honesty means telling the truth and being fair, even when it's hard.

What Happens Next?

The police and other helpers in Nigeria will keep looking for Mr. Mamman. They want to make sure that everyone, no matter how big their job, follows the rules and faces the consequences if they don't. This story reminds us that even when bad things happen, people are working hard to make sure fairness wins in the end. So, let's all remember to always be fair, share our toys, and tell the truth. That makes our homes, our schools, and our world a happy and bright place for everyone! Thank you for listening, super friends!
summary: A former Power Minister in Nigeria, Mr. Saleh Mamman, was found guilty of stealing money meant for electricity and sentenced to 75 years, but he is currently missing and people are looking for him.
whyMatters: This story matters because it teaches us that everyone, even big leaders, must follow the rules and be honest, and that taking money meant to help everyone is very, very wrong.

title: 'They shot my neighbour in the head' - the lakeside city traumatised by war
content: Rebel fighters and Rwandan troops are accused of committing atrocities after capturing the DR Congo city of Uvira in December.

originalTitle: Mahmoud Khalil calls for deportation to be halted in light of new evidence
title: A Student's Big Wish: To Stay and Learn!
content:
Hello, Super Reporters!

Hello, amazing friends! It’s your kid-friendly reporter here, ready with a very special story for you from far, far away. This story is about a young man named Mahmoud Khalil. He is a student, just like some of you will be when you go to bigger schools, maybe even at a university! Mahmoud was studying very hard at a big school called Columbia. It's in a country very far from Africa. He loved to learn new things and meet new people. But Mahmoud had a big worry, a really big one. He was told that he might have to leave the country where he was studying. This is called 'deportation' (dee-por-TAY-shun). It means being asked to go back to your home country. Imagine if you were playing with your friends in a new place, and someone told you it was time to leave forever, even if you wanted to stay!

Meet Mahmoud and His Worry

Mahmoud Khalil is a kind student. He cares a lot about important things in the world. Sometimes, he spoke up about things he believed in, like helping people in a place called Palestine. This is part of what students do – they learn and they think about making the world a better place. Because of some things that happened, Mahmoud was facing this 'deportation'. It made him very sad. He wanted to finish his studies and continue learning and growing. Being asked to leave was like a big storm cloud over his head. Did you know? A student is someone who goes to school to learn. There are millions of students all over Africa, just like you! Learning helps us understand the world better.

What Are Lawyers?

But Mahmoud is not alone! He has some very clever helpers called lawyers (LAW-yers). Lawyers are like super-detectives and rule-keepers all rolled into one! They know all about the rules of a country, which we call 'laws'. When someone has a problem with the rules, or if they need help speaking up for themselves, lawyers are there. They listen very carefully to the story and try to find the best way to help. They are like a friend who knows all the rules to a game and helps you play fairly. Mahmoud’s lawyers are working very hard for him. They want to make sure he gets a fair chance. They believe everyone should be treated fairly, no matter where they are from or what they believe. Did you know? Lawyers often go to school for many, many years to learn all the laws. It's a very important job!

Finding New Clues: 'New Evidence'

Now, here is where the story gets really interesting! Mahmoud’s lawyers found something very important. They found 'new evidence'. Imagine you are playing a treasure hunt, and you thought you knew where the treasure was, but then you find a brand new map that shows a different way! That's what 'new evidence' is. It’s new information, new facts, or new clues that were not known before. These new clues can change how people think about a problem. Mahmoud's lawyers looked very, very carefully at everything that happened before. They dug deep, just like you might dig for special stones in the ground. And they found these important new clues about his case. This new evidence makes them think that maybe, just maybe, things were not done fairly the first time. It's like finding out someone changed the rules of your game without telling anyone.

What Does 'Engineered' Mean?

The lawyers say this new evidence suggests that a group of people, from a government team many years ago, might have 'engineered' (EN-jin-eerd) the outcome. This is a big word, but it means something simple. To 'engineer' something means to plan it very carefully to make it happen in a certain way. Sometimes, people engineer things to build a bridge or a car – that's good! But sometimes, people engineer things in a way that is not fair to others. In Mahmoud's case, the lawyers are worried that this old government team planned things so that Mahmoud would be asked to leave, even if it wasn't the fairest way to decide. It's like if someone secretly made sure you lost a game, even if you were playing well. Did you know? Governments are groups of people who help run a country and make sure rules are followed. They have many important jobs!

Why is Being Fair So Important?

Think about playing with your friends. What happens if someone doesn't play fairly? Maybe they cheat, or they change the rules just for them. It doesn't feel good, right? It makes you feel sad or angry. Being fair means everyone gets a chance. It means rules are the same for everyone. It means listening to all sides of a story before making a decision. It’s a very important idea for all people, all over the world, including right here in Africa. Mahmoud’s lawyers are saying that because of this 'new evidence', the way Mahmoud's case was handled might not have been fair. And if it wasn't fair, then the decision to send him away might not be right either.

A Big Ask: Stop the Sending Away!

So, what are Mahmoud’s lawyers asking for? They are asking for the 'deportation' to be 'halted'. To 'halt' means to stop something right now. Like when you are running and someone says 'halt!' and you stop. They want the decision to send Mahmoud away to be stopped. They want the people who make these big decisions to look at the 'new evidence' very carefully. They want them to think again. They hope that if everyone looks at the new information, they will see that Mahmoud deserves another chance. They believe he should be allowed to stay and finish his studies, and keep helping others. This is a big wish, but it's a wish for fairness. It's a wish that everyone gets a chance to explain their side of the story and that old mistakes can be made right.

Our Wishes for Mahmoud

We all wish for Mahmoud Khalil to be treated with fairness and kindness. We hope that the people in charge will listen to his lawyers and look at the new clues. We hope Mahmoud can continue to learn and grow. Every person deserves a fair chance, and every student deserves to learn. Let's send our good wishes to Mahmoud and his lawyers as they work hard to make things right. It reminds us all to always play fair and be kind to everyone, no matter where they come from!
summary: Mahmoud Khalil, a student facing deportation, has new evidence through his lawyers suggesting his case was unfairly handled, and they want him to be allowed to stay.
whyMatters: This story matters because it teaches us that everyone deserves to be treated fairly, and that sometimes new information can help make a wrong decision right again.
